topic: An employee finds out a junior colleague makes more money
raw_output:
[story 1]

Elias stared at the glowing screen, the numbers blurring into meaningless digits. He hadn't touched his lukewarm coffee in twenty minutes, the mug growing cold in his hands. The realization hit him with a dull thud: Sarah, the new intern, was earning significantly more than he did, despite doing what felt like half the work. He shifted in his ergonomic chair, the squeak of the wheels echoing loudly in the otherwise quiet office. A strange, heavy emptiness settled in his chest, making it hard to draw a full breath. He watched Sarah confidently stride past his cubicle, a bright, energetic contrast to his own slow, dragging movements. He picked up his pen, but the ink seemed to swim on the page, and he just let it drop back into the tray.

[story 2]

"Another spreadsheet," Mark muttered, rubbing his temples as he stared at the quarterly reports. The fluorescent lights above hummed with an irritating, persistent buzz. He felt a profound inertia, a weight pressing down on his shoulders that made lifting his arms feel like an Olympic feat. Then he saw the figures. Sarah’s salary, displayed on the internal HR portal, was a stark, undeniable contrast to his own meager compensation. A slow, tight knot formed in his stomach. He cleared his throat, the sound dry and raspy. He didn't look up, just traced a pattern on his desk with a fingertip, his gaze fixed on the wood grain, wishing the floor would simply swallow him whole.

[story 3]

The afternoon sun slanted across the dusty floor of the archive room, illuminating motes of disturbed air. David leaned against a shelf, his posture slumped, his eyes unfocused. He’d been trying to concentrate on cataloging old files, but his mind kept drifting back to the pay stubs he’d accidentally glimpsed. Sarah’s earnings were astronomical compared to his own. A strange numbness crept over him, a feeling of detachment from the tangible world around him. He picked up a brittle document, his fingers moving mechanically, without any real engagement. He felt heavy, anchored to the spot, as if the effort required to move even an inch was monumental.
